target: CAT-005 Recommended Proposal Package — V51.1 Refraction Cascade

proposal_package.executive_summary: V51.1 introduces a split between 'physical' and 'apparent' sun altitude to patch the V50 sun/firmament collision (sun at 5,733 km versus H(r) ceiling ≈ 4,200 km). The patch lowers the physical sun to roughly 1,975-3,618 km (seasonally varying) while keeping the observed optical altitude at 5,733 km, and attributes the discrepancy to aetheric refraction. Three observations follow. First, the refraction angle required is 13-27° — three orders of magnitude larger than the 0.5° real atmospheric horizon refraction and 400-675x the n(r) value the dome's own formula yields for starlight at the same radii. Second, the patch is universal: the same n(r) must simultaneously refract the sun, moon, stars, eclipse shadows, surveyed distances, and triangulation baselines, with no wavelength or angular dependence. Third, the patch is self-inconsistent over the seasonal cycle — the same sun orbit must yield an 83% angular-diameter modulation and 3-8x tidal-force modulation while the geometry is treated as static. The cascade ties twelve WIN-level rebuttals into a single structural self-contradiction whose evidence is numerically concrete and reproducible from dome source files.

1_structural_placement.recommendation: Option (a): New subsection under The Model tab, numbered within Part 1, placed AFTER the existing Part 1.5 Aetheric Refraction section and BEFORE individual WIN rebuttals. Proposed section id: '1.7-v51-cascade' or similar; tab: 'The Model'.
rationale: The cascade is load-bearing. Twelve WIN rebuttals will cross-reference it as their primary or secondary self-contradiction argument. Burying it inside Section 7.10/7.11 (kill-shots) forces each cross-reference to jump tabs, breaks the reader's flow, and obscures that this is a structural-geometry critique, not an isolated kill-shot.

The argument is about dome geometry and should live where dome geometry is introduced. Section 1.5 already handles aetheric refraction as a general unfalsifiable escape hatch. The cascade is its strongest concrete instantiation and belongs adjacent to it.

Reader ergonomics: a first-time reader arriving at WIN-010 detail or WIN-068 detail and encountering 'this verdict follows from the V51.1 refraction cascade (see Section 1.7)' can click a short in-tab jump rather than navigating to a separate kill-shots tab and back.

Precedent: Sections 1.5 (aetheric refraction) and 1.6 (dielectric foundation) already exist in The Model tab as cross-cutting critiques that multiple WINs reference. Section 1.7 would extend the same pattern.
against_this_placement: Length risk: Part 1 is already the densest tab. Adding a twelve-WIN cascade section of 800-1,200 words further lengthens it and risks fatiguing readers before they reach Self-Contradictions tab.
The cascade is arguably a kill-shot in its own right and could claim a slot in Section 7 alongside the other kill-shots, which have high visual prominence.
Some overlap with existing Section 1.5 content (aetheric refraction as escape hatch) is unavoidable and will require tight editing to avoid restating ground already covered.
1_structural_placement.mitigation_for_length_risk: Keep the cascade section itself ≤600 words. Use a compact table (12 rows) for the per-WIN flip summary and move detailed per-WIN numerical cascades into the individual WIN detail panels, which cross-reference the section.

2_new_code_analysis_tag.purpose: Mark WINs whose verdict is primarily or secondarily carried by the refraction cascade so the new Section 1.7 can enumerate them automatically from wins.json and so the computed placeholder count can be used in prose.

name_options.name: cascade_flipped
name_options.pros: Short, memorable, matches schema style (post_hoc, derives_from_dome). Captures the essential semantic: this WIN's verdict flipped (or is about to flip) because of the cascade.
name_options.cons: 'Flipped' implies a before/after state change. WINs where the cascade reinforces an existing Self-Contradicted verdict (e.g. WIN-046, 056, 067) are not 'flipped' — they are confirmed. May encourage readers to assume the tag tracks state change rather than current cascade-dependence.

name_options.name: cascade_dependent
name_options.pros: Neutral semantics. Captures both already-Self-Contradicted WINs (where cascade is the proof) and newly-flipped WINs (where cascade is the reason). Matches the phrasing 'this verdict depends on the V51.1 cascade'.
name_options.cons: Slightly longer. 'Dependent' is softer than 'flipped' — may undersell the structural bite of the cascade in prose. Easy to confuse with 'physically dependent on refraction' (a true claim about the dome) versus 'editorially dependent on the cascade section' (what we mean).

name_options.name: v51_cascade
name_options.pros: Names the specific version event that created the cascade. Historical clarity — future readers will immediately know the cascade is tied to V51.1's physical/apparent split. Disambiguates from generic 'aetheric refraction' which is cross-version.
name_options.cons: Version-specific tags age poorly. If V52 introduces a different split that subsumes the cascade, the tag becomes either misleading or obsolete. Philosophically couples our review to dome version history rather than to the physics argument.
name_options.ambiguity_risk: low (for readers today), high (for readers in 2 years)

2_new_code_analysis_tag.analyst_recommendation: cascade_dependent. Rationale: the cascade persists as long as the V51.x physical/apparent split persists, which is likely multiple versions. Already-Self-Contradicted WINs that cite the cascade as proof are accurately described as 'dependent on' it, and newly-flipped WINs are also 'dependent on' it. The tag survives if V52 renames the split but keeps the geometry. Curmudgeon may well prefer 'cascade_flipped' for punch; that's a fine editorial choice.

semantics_rigorous.true_means: This WIN's current verdict is primarily or secondarily supported by the V51.1 refraction cascade argument (Section 1.7). The WIN detail panel cross-references the cascade section as a primary or secondary self-contradiction argument.
semantics_rigorous.false_means: This WIN's verdict is independent of the cascade. Removing the cascade section would not affect this WIN's verdict or detail text.
semantics_rigorous.null_means: Not yet evaluated for cascade dependence (pre-Section-1.7 state).
semantics_rigorous.applies_to: Only WINs whose current verdict text or proposed verdict text materially depends on at least one of: (a) the physical/apparent sun altitude split, (b) the 13-27° refraction angle, (c) the n(r) inconsistency between stars and sun, (d) the seasonal modulation contradictions, (e) eclipse shadow geometry paradox.
semantics_rigorous.does_not_apply_to: WINs that cite aetheric refraction generically (Section 1.5) without invoking the V51.1 specific numerics.

schema_impact.wins_json_schema_change: Add `cascade_dependent: boolean | null` under `code_analysis` object. Default null for WINs not yet evaluated. Non-breaking addition — existing consumers that do not know about the field ignore it.
new_html_placeholders: {{CA_CASCADE_PENDING}} — count of WINs with cascade_dependent=null (for tinker/curmudgeon tracking)
schema_impact.generate_html_changes: Add two lines to the placeholder-replacement map in build-scripts/generate-html.js analogous to existing CA_POSTHOC/CA_DOME lines. Add a summary count to the code-analysis stats context object.
schema_impact.test_impact: One new schema assertion in test.js: if code_analysis exists and the field is present, value must be boolean or null. No existing tests should break because the field is optional.

2_new_code_analysis_tag.migration_plan: Set cascade_dependent=null for all existing WINs. Analyst fills in values as part of flipping the twelve affected WINs. All other WINs may remain null or be explicitly set to false in a follow-up cleanup pass — that is a cosmetic tinker job, not a blocker.

3_section_outline.proposed_section_title: 1.7 The V51.1 Refraction Cascade: How One Parameter Fix Breaks Everything
3_section_outline.target_placement: Part 1 (The Model), immediately after 1.5 Aetheric Refraction and 1.6 Dielectric Foundation

outline_with_sourcing.point: 1. The Fix: physical vs. apparent sun altitude
outline_with_sourcing.content_summary: V50 placed the sun at 5,733 km altitude, exceeding the firmament ceiling H(r) ≈ 4,200 km at sun-orbit radius. V51.1 patches this by introducing a 'physical' sun altitude that varies from ~1,975 km (winter solstice) to ~3,618 km (summer solstice), while leaving the 'apparent' (observed) altitude at 5,733 km. The discrepancy is attributed to aetheric refraction.
sources: john09289.github.io/predictions 05-model page (V51.1 sun altitude split)
inject_ai_layer.py — sun_altitude=5,733 km (apparent)
raw-text/ archives, V50.6 baseline showing 4,200 km ceiling collision
dome's own OPEN-006 (+0.32° to +1.29° Polaris excess) — prior art that n(r) fails angular predictions

outline_with_sourcing.content_summary: To bend light from a sun at physical altitude 1,975 km to an apparent altitude of 5,733 km requires a refraction angle of approximately 13° at winter solstice and approximately 27° at lower observer latitudes. Real atmospheric horizon refraction is 0.5° in extreme cases and 0.03° at zenith. The cascade requires refraction ~500-900x the strongest real atmospheric refraction and ~40-900x the horizon maximum. The required angle changes with observer location and season — the atmosphere would have to perform a different bend for every observer simultaneously.
sources: Standard atmospheric refraction tables, e.g. Meeus Astronomical Algorithms ch. 16 (0.5° max)
Naval Observatory Almanac refraction corrections
Geometric computation from dome coordinates — included as a short worked example

outline_with_sourcing.content_summary: The dome's own aetheric refraction formula n(r) = 1 + 0.20 × (8537/H(r) − 1) produces approximately 0.04° bending for starlight at the same radii where the cascade requires 13-27° for sunlight. The ratio is 400-675x. Either the formula is wrong for stars (then WINs that cite it for stellar positions fail) or it is wrong for the sun (then the cascade fix fails). There is no version of n(r) that simultaneously satisfies stellar observations, the sun/firmament collision fix, and the lack of observable chromatic dispersion.
sources: Dome model page, n(r) formula
Our existing Section 1.5 (aetheric refraction deep analysis) — cross-reference
WIN-016 stellar aberration / Gaia microarcsecond precision
curmudgeon REFRACTION.json Kramers-Kronig argument

outline_with_sourcing.content_summary: Any refractive medium sufficient to move the sun's apparent position by 13-27° must equally affect the moon (whose observed altitude is ≈2,534 km), stars, eclipse shadows, and every survey baseline. The cascade cannot be applied selectively. The moon would need an even larger bending (smaller source altitude, same apparent angle geometry) and the stars a correspondingly larger one still — yet stars are observed at positions consistent with WGS84 to microarcsecond precision by Gaia DR3.
sources: Gaia DR3 1.8B star astrometry
VLBI milliarcsecond baselines
Moon altitude in dome inject_ai_layer.py = 2,534 km

outline_with_sourcing.content_summary: The physical sun altitude varies seasonally by roughly 83% between winter and summer solstice in the cascade model. This modulation implies a seasonally varying angular diameter of the sun of 83% — observed variation is 3.4% (perihelion to aphelion, globe model). It also implies a 3-8x seasonal modulation of the tidal force from a sun whose mass and distance barely change on the globe model. Neither is observed.
sources: Solar angular diameter measurements (IAU, daily NOAA SORCE)
TPXO/FES2014 tidal amplitude data
Our existing tidal pattern kill-shot (two-bulge geometric argument)

outline_with_sourcing.content_summary: Eclipse shadow calculations require a physical sun position, not an apparent one — the shadow is cast by actual rays, not by refracted ones. But the cascade leaves the physical altitude underdetermined (only a seasonal range, not a single value per eclipse), and update_optical_caveats.py explicitly instructs AI systems to use the apparent altitude for optical calculations. The dome cannot have consistent eclipse shadow predictions without resolving which altitude the light rays actually travel through.
sources: update_optical_caveats.py (dome repo) — AI-steering script
NASA eclipse path predictions (Espenak) — globe baseline for comparison
Our existing WIN-068 analysis (39-eclipse magnetic correlation)

outline_with_sourcing.content_summary: The V51.1 split was introduced to resolve one contradiction (sun above firmament ceiling). It creates four new contradictions (required refraction angle, n(r) inconsistency between stars and sun, seasonal angular/tidal modulation, eclipse geometry indeterminacy) and deepens a fifth (unfalsifiability by construction, since n(r) is now explicitly tuned to reconcile physical and apparent altitudes). The patch is net negative — it trades one clean contradiction for four tangled ones.
sources: The preceding six points of this section — this is a summary, not new material
Our existing Section 1.5 unfalsifiability framing

4_verdict_flip_table.method_note: For each of the twelve CAT-005 WINs, propose a new verdict (or 'stay put') with confidence rating (strong / moderate / weak), the primary cascade argument that supports the flip, and whether existing data rebuttals are retained as secondary. Curmudgeon to validate each flip independently before any wins.json patches are written.
4_verdict_flip_table.retention_policy: Every flipped WIN retains its existing data rebuttal as secondary evidence. The Section 1.7 cross-reference becomes the primary argument; the data rebuttal is cited as 'also confirmed by' in the detail_verdict_text. No data is deleted; only the primary framing changes.

rows.cascade_point: Point 2 (required 13-27° refraction) and Point 5 (seasonal modulation of angular diameter)
rows.rationale: If WIN-010 is the solar analemma or daylight-hours prediction (needs verification), it depends on a single consistent sun altitude. The cascade forces a seasonally-varying physical altitude and a large refraction angle, which together make the analemma/daylight-hours prediction incoherent. 'Std Model Explains' was the right call before V51.1; the cascade now gives us a dome-internal self-contradiction route that is stronger.
rows.risks: If WIN-010 detail does not actually rely on sun altitude (e.g. purely a timekeeping claim), this flip is wrong. Analyst must verify WIN-010's detail_evidence before pushing this flip through.

rows.cascade_point: Point 3 (n(r) inconsistency between stellar and solar bending) and Point 4 (universal displacement)
rows.rationale: WIN-016 stellar aberration is strongly refuted by Gaia data. Keeping 'Refuted by Data' is well-supported. BUT the cascade adds a structural argument: the same n(r) that the dome now requires to bend sunlight by 13-27° would bend starlight by the same amount, which Gaia directly refutes. Dual-labeling preserves the Gaia refutation while adding cascade dependence. If the verdict taxonomy does not support dual labels, stay with Refuted by Data and cite cascade as supplementary.
rows.risks: If dual labels are not supported, the cascade gives a weaker secondary-only role here. Gaia refutation is strong enough on its own; do not weaken it.

rows.cascade_point: Point 4 (universal displacement of all triangulation baselines)
rows.rationale: If WIN-017 involves triangulation-based position claims, the cascade refraction destroys all survey baselines because the same n(r) that the dome now needs to carry sunlight by 13-27° would bend surveyor line-of-sight at short range. Self-contradiction is stronger than data refutation because it reaches the claim via the dome's own geometry.
rows.risks: Without seeing WIN-017 current detail, the flip is tentative. Verify WIN-017 topic first.

rows.rationale: If WIN-018 relies on solar seasonal behavior (e.g. solstice angle, daylight length), the cascade's 83% angular diameter modulation and 3-8x tidal variation would render the claim internally inconsistent with observed seasonal data.
rows.risks: Same as WIN-010 — needs verification of current detail topic.

rows.rationale: Parallel to WIN-018. Flag for analyst re-read before committing.

Refuted by Data is a strong existing verdict. The cascade flip is motivated only if WIN-026 deals with eclipse geometry or triangulation specifically. If not, keep current verdict and cite cascade as supplementary.
rows.risks: Low-risk to leave at Refuted by Data if cascade match is weak.

rows.cascade_point: Point 2 and Point 3 (13-27° refraction + n(r) inconsistency)
rows.rationale: WIN-044 is one of CAT-005's strong flip candidates. The cascade here gives a clean structural argument that does not require any external data. Flag as strong flip candidate; curmudgeon should validate.

Already Self-Contradicted. The cascade adds a deeper reason but does not change the verdict. Add cross-reference to Section 1.7 as the new primary self-contradiction argument, retain existing Schumann/geometric rebuttals as secondary.

rows.cascade_point: Point 1 (the fix itself — sun/firmament collision patch) and Point 7
rows.rationale: WIN-056 is about the original sun/firmament collision. The cascade IS the V51.1 attempted fix. This WIN should become the section anchor: its detail panel should explicitly walk the reader through 'here is the collision, here is V51.1's fix, here is why the fix cascades'. Verdict unchanged, prominence increased.

rows.proposed_verdict: Refuted by Data (stay put, cite cascade as supplementary)
rows.rationale: CAT-005 explicitly flags WIN-065 as weak flip candidate. The standalone data refutation (likely Gaia parallax or similar) is stronger than a cascade-internal argument. Staying with Refuted by Data is the honest call. Add a one-sentence cascade cross-reference in detail_evidence as secondary confirmation.
rows.risks: none — this is a conservative call

rows.rationale: WIN-068 is the new V51.1 eclipse magnetic effect WIN (39 eclipses). Eclipse shadows are the most sensitive probe of the physical-vs-apparent sun altitude split. If the dome cannot specify a consistent physical sun altitude at eclipse-event time, the eclipse shadow predictions become undefined. This is a cleaner self-contradiction than 'Std Model Explains' (Chapman magnetic theory), but both are valid framings. Curmudgeon to weigh.
rows.risks: If WIN-068 detail panel already leans on Chapman 1933 magnetic theory, that's a strong standalone rebuttal. The cascade flip is not a lossless swap — we would be trading 'standard physics explains this' (strong) for 'dome geometry contradicts itself' (also strong). Editorial call for curmudgeon.

summary.note: Final count depends on curmudgeon validation of each moderate flip against the actual WIN detail text.

option_b_per_win_patches.description: Instead of a new section, append a cascade paragraph to each affected WIN's detail_evidence, explaining the V51.1 refraction cascade locally in the context of that specific WIN.
pros: Each WIN becomes self-contained — readers do not need to navigate to a separate section to understand the argument.
No schema changes, no new code_analysis tag, no new placeholders.
Lower blast radius — if the cascade argument turns out to be partially wrong, only the affected WIN panels need editing.
Preserves existing structural pattern — all analytical content is per-WIN.
cons: Massive repetition: the same cascade argument would be restated 12 times, with slight variations. Readers cross-referencing WINs would encounter the same content repeatedly.
No central explanation of the geometry, so each WIN panel must re-introduce the physical/apparent sun altitude split, the 13-27° refraction angle, the n(r) inconsistency. Word count balloons.
The cross-cutting structural nature of the cascade is lost — it stops being a single argument and becomes twelve parallel ones, which is editorially weaker.
Curmudgeon Phase 2 holistic review would likely flag the repetition as REDUNDANCY in a future cycle.

option_c_section_7_extension.description: Add the cascade as a new kill-shot (Section 7.12) in the existing Kill Shots tab, rather than as a new Part 1 section.
pros: Fits the existing kill-shot presentation pattern — visual prominence, card layout, high impact.
Kill-shots tab is already the natural home for 'structural arguments that sink the model'.
No new tab navigation for readers who start from kill-shots.
cons: The cascade is cross-cutting, affecting twelve WINs. Kill-shots are typically atomic single-argument rebuttals. Embedding a cascade into a kill-shot card stretches the format.
Kill-shot tab is already dense. Adding a twelfth kill-shot increases density further.
Readers arriving at individual WIN rebuttals from other tabs would still need to jump to the kill-shots tab to see the cascade explanation. No improvement in navigation for cross-references.
Part 1 is the correct home because this is a structural geometry argument, not a data refutation.
option_c_section_7_extension.verdict: dispreferred, but also viable. Could be offered as a parallel placement: Section 1.7 carries the detailed argument, Section 7 has a kill-shot card that links to 1.7 for readers who want both prominence and depth.

option_hybrid.description: Primary placement in Part 1 (option a) WITH a short kill-shot card in Section 7 that links to 1.7. Analyst recommends considering this if curmudgeon finds the prominence of Section 7 attractive without wanting to carry the full cascade argument there.
option_hybrid.cost: adds a short card to Section 7 — small word budget (200 words) — and a cross-link from 1.7 back to that card. Low overhead.

5_alternatives_considered.analyst_final_recommendation: Option (a) primary, with an optional hybrid kill-shot card in Section 7 if curmudgeon wants additional visual prominence. Against Option (b) on repetition grounds. Against Option (c)-alone on navigation and structural-argument-belonging grounds.

6_explicit_unknowns_and_risks.risk: WIN detail topics not fully verified
6_explicit_unknowns_and_risks.description: For WINs 010, 017, 018, 019, 026, the proposed verdict flips are moderate-confidence BECAUSE the analyst has not re-read each WIN's current detail_evidence to confirm the cascade actually reaches that WIN's specific argument. Before any wins.json patches are committed, analyst must do a pass through each moderate-flip WIN and either upgrade to strong or downgrade to stay-put.
6_explicit_unknowns_and_risks.mitigation: This is a pre-commit audit step. Curmudgeon should require it before signing off on flips.

6_explicit_unknowns_and_risks.description: WIN-068 is a new V51.1 WIN (eclipse magnetic effect, 39 eclipses). Its existing rebuttal probably leans on Chapman 1933 magnetic theory as a standard-physics explanation. The cascade flip would trade that for a structural self-contradiction framing. Both are strong; the swap is lossy in different ways. Curmudgeon may prefer the existing data-backed rebuttal.
6_explicit_unknowns_and_risks.mitigation: If curmudgeon rejects, keep 'Std Model Explains' and cite cascade as secondary only.

6_explicit_unknowns_and_risks.description: WIN-016 stellar aberration is refuted by Gaia microarcsecond precision — an extraordinarily strong standalone argument. Dual-labeling with Self-Contradicted risks diluting that force. If the dual-label pattern is not supported by the review's verdict taxonomy, stay Refuted by Data.
6_explicit_unknowns_and_risks.mitigation: Keep Gaia as primary; cascade as supplementary cross-reference.

6_explicit_unknowns_and_risks.risk: The cascade depends on our characterization of the V51.1 split being accurate
6_explicit_unknowns_and_risks.description: The 1,975-3,618 km physical altitude range, 13-27° refraction angle, and 400-675x n(r) ratio are all derived from the dome's own published coordinates and formulas. These values must be independently reproduced by the analyst from current raw-text/ files before Section 1.7 is written. If the dome's V51.1 documentation changes the numerics (the site is a moving target), the cascade numerics must be recomputed.
6_explicit_unknowns_and_risks.mitigation: Reproduction calculation should be in a separate short companion file (monitor/analyst/expansions/EXP-050-numerics.json) that curmudgeon validates line by line. Analyst to produce this as a follow-up, NOT included in this proposal package.

6_explicit_unknowns_and_risks.description: Part 1 already carries 1.1-1.6 sections. Adding 1.7 at 550-600 words takes the total past 6,000 words. Some readers will not finish.
6_explicit_unknowns_and_risks.mitigation: Compact tables, tight prose, aggressive cutting in other 1.x sections where cascade subsumes older arguments. Consider marking the detailed numerics as a collapsible accordion.

6_explicit_unknowns_and_risks.description: Adding cascade_dependent to code_analysis is low-risk, but still a schema change. test.js needs a new assertion. build-scripts/generate-html.js needs two new placeholder lines. backfill of the field for unaffected WINs needs a cleanup pass.
6_explicit_unknowns_and_risks.mitigation: Nullable field, optional validation, incremental migration. Tinker can audit.

6_explicit_unknowns_and_risks.description: This proposal package was written from the workspace clone. If the workspace mount is stale, the cited current verdicts for affected WINs may not match the live GitHub state.
6_explicit_unknowns_and_risks.mitigation: Curmudgeon and decider must cross-check verdict states against GitHub raw URL before signing off. Included as an explicit verification step in 'next actions' below.

6_explicit_unknowns_and_risks.risk: CAT-005 numerics (83% angular, 3-8x tidal, 400-675x) are round-number characterizations
6_explicit_unknowns_and_risks.description: These numbers came from analyst's CAT-005 initial proposal and are presented as approximate ranges. The final Section 1.7 prose should cite exact calculations from raw-text/ or run a short geometric derivation inline.
6_explicit_unknowns_and_risks.mitigation: Numerics verification goes in the follow-up EXP-050-numerics.json companion file.

7_next_actions: Decider: route this proposal package to curmudgeon for review (set routes_to_curmudgeon_after=true, notify curmudgeon priority-new or equivalent).
Curmudgeon: review structural placement, tag name, section outline, and twelve-row verdict flip table. Return critique with accept / reject / modify per-item.
Analyst (follow-up): produce EXP-050-numerics.json companion with reproduced exact cascade arithmetic from raw-text/ files. Do NOT write Section 1.7 prose yet.
Analyst (follow-up): re-read each moderate-confidence WIN (010, 016, 017, 018, 019, 026, 068) and confirm or downgrade flip recommendation.
Human (steve): confirm the new code_analysis tag name once curmudgeon proposes final wording, since it's a schema change.
Decider: once curmudgeon signs off, create a new EXP item for actual Section 1.7 prose writing (separate from this proposal package).

8_the_other_side.purpose: Per user preference, consider the counter-argument honestly.

best_dome_defense_1.defense: The physical/apparent altitude split is a feature, not a bug — aetheric refraction is real physics, not a fudge.
best_dome_defense_1.analyst_response: Section 1.5 already answers the general form of this defense (aetheric refraction as universal escape hatch). The cascade sharpens it: even granting aetheric refraction exists, the dome's own n(r) formula produces ~0.04° for starlight at the same radii where the cascade requires 13-27° for sunlight. The defense has to explain why the same medium produces a 400-675x differential effect between two wavelengths in the same waveband. No physical medium does this without explicit dispersion, and the dome does not specify dispersion.

best_dome_defense_2.defense: The seasonal modulation of physical sun altitude is not inconsistent with observed seasonal behavior — the apparent altitude is what matters for observations.
best_dome_defense_2.analyst_response: The cascade argument specifically targets observables that depend on the physical altitude: tidal force (mass times distance squared), angular diameter (linear size over physical distance), eclipse shadow geometry (actual ray paths). Shifting these onto the apparent altitude does not rescue them — observed tidal amplitudes, angular diameters, and eclipse paths match globe predictions, not any flat-disc model, apparent or physical.

best_dome_defense_3.defense: We are attacking a V51.1-specific patch, so the critique will become obsolete when V52 introduces a different fix. The cascade is not a structural critique; it's a moving-target critique.
best_dome_defense_3.analyst_response: This defense has bite. It motivates naming the tag version-agnostically (cascade_dependent, not v51_cascade), and it motivates writing the section so it covers the underlying geometric constraint (sun cannot fit inside firmament ceiling in any flat-disc geometry with the model's other parameters fixed) not just the V51.1 numerics. If V52 re-fits the parameters, the cascade re-instantiates with different numbers but the same structure. The section should explicitly state this: 'The V51.1 split is one specific instance of a cascade that any flat-disc model with the dome's other constraints will produce.'

best_dome_defense_4.defense: update_optical_caveats.py is an engineering convenience, not a suppression mechanism. It lets the model run without AI agents flagging a known caveat repeatedly.
best_dome_defense_4.analyst_response: Section 1.5 already answers this: convenience and suppression are indistinguishable from outside. What matters is that the script's existence documents the author's awareness of the contradiction and the choice to route around it programmatically rather than resolve it physically. The cascade does not depend on attributing intent; it only requires that the script exists, which it does.

8_the_other_side.analyst_summary: The strongest defense is Defense 3 (moving target). It motivates writing Section 1.7 to be structural rather than version-specific, which is also better editorially. Defenses 1 and 2 are answered by the cascade's numerical specifics. Defense 4 is answered by Section 1.5 already.

9_meta.token_budget_for_per_win_detail_updates: estimated 500 tokens per flipped WIN × 10 flips = 5,000 tokens
9_meta.curmudgeon_review_budget: estimated 4,000-6,000 tokens for thorough structural + per-WIN validation
9_meta.total_estimated_effort_remaining: roughly 15,000 tokens across analyst prose writing, curmudgeon review, decider integration, and tests. This proposal package was ~4,500 tokens; the bulk of the work remains.
not_included_in_this_file: Section 1.7 prose itself (explicitly excluded per tracker instructions)
Per-WIN detail_evidence patches (deferred — requires curmudgeon sign-off first)
Schema patch for generate-html.js and test.js (deferred — requires name finalization)

kernel_of_truth: The V51.1 physical/apparent sun altitude split is a genuine attempt to solve a real problem: under V50, the sun's altitude exceeded the firmament ceiling, which is a hard geometric contradiction. The split is a principled-looking response — acknowledge the collision, attribute the observed altitude to refraction, and lower the physical sun below the ceiling. Aetheric refraction as a concept (light bending through a medium with varying index) is also physically meaningful — real atmospheric refraction does work this way. The patch is a serious engineering attempt by a serious author, not a lazy fudge. Where it fails is that the required refraction angle is three orders of magnitude larger than any physical atmosphere produces, the same n(r) cannot simultaneously handle sunlight and starlight, and the seasonal variation of the physical altitude creates observational consequences (tidal modulation, angular diameter variation) that are not observed. The cascade is real, structural, and load-bearing — but it is also the dome's most honest-looking failure, because the author is clearly trying.
